doom.driver: Tensions flare near Strait of Hormuz as a ship is seized and another is sunk.
doom.blurb: HORMUZ HEATS UP: SHIPS DON'T COME BACK
doom.summary: Iran just seized a floating armoury ship and sank another near Oman, which is a bold escalation given the ongoing standoff that's already strangled global energy flows. India is already hiking fuel prices as the crisis bites, the UAE is panic-building a second oil bypass pipeline, and markets are dumping bonds, stocks, and silver simultaneously — the financial system's version of 'every man for himself.' The Trump-Xi summit concluded with pageantry, vague vibes, and zero breakthroughs on Iran, meaning the world's two largest economies watched the Strait of Hormuz get messier and agreed to disagree about it.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Active Iranian ship seizures in the Strait of Hormuz represent a concrete escalation beyond posturing, with India raising fuel prices and the UAE fast-tracking a bypass pipeline as economic shockwaves spread.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Trump-Xi summit ended without Iran deal or Taiwan resolution, leaving two of the world's most dangerous flashpoints on a slow boil with no diplomatic off-ramp in sight.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: OpenAI's Codex hits mobile, arXiv is banning researchers for AI-hallucinated citations, and Ontario audits confirm AI doctors are fabricating prescriptions — capability racing ahead of reliability on multiple fronts.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: A new Ebola strain is suspected in an active outbreak that has killed 65 in eastern DRC, with 246 reported cases and African health officials scrambling to coordinate containment.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Pope Leo XIV explicitly warned that AI-directed warfare leads to a 'spiral of annihilation' — a signal that concern is going mainstream, even if the systems themselves remain controllable for now.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — night scene, aftermath composition, camera at low oblique angle over dark Gulf of Oman water northeast of Fujairah, artificial light only, deep navy and smoldering orange palette — the water surface is black and glassy, broken only by the scattered orange reflections of a distant fire still smoldering on the horizon where the Haji Ali went down, a thick column of dark smoke rising and bending slowly in the windless pre-dawn heat, backlit from below by dying ember-glow — in the foreground, a cluster of orange life rafts drift loose and empty on the black water, their reflective strips catching faint firelight, rope trailing into the dark — a white-hulled Omani coast guard patrol vessel sits motionless to the left, deck lights on, crew visible as small dark silhouettes standing at the rail looking toward the smoke, the vessel's navigation lights casting red and green pools across the water — to the right, a large anchored cargo ship sits in darkness, no movement aboard, anchor chain descending into black water, a single floodlight on its stern illuminating an empty deck — the sky above is hazy amber-brown, dust-filtered, no stars visible, only the smear of distant port lights from Fujairah terminal on the far horizon, white cylindrical storage tanks barely discernible through the haze — heavy shadows dominate, warmth feels threatening.

dawn.driver: Scientists 'bottle the sun' with a liquid battery that stores solar energy. Scientists at UC Santa Barbara have created a remarkable new material that works like a 'rechargeable solar battery,' storing sunlight inside tiny molecules and releasing it later as heat — even long after the sun has set.
dawn.blurb: SUNLIGHT IN A BOTTLE, HEAT ON DEMAND
dawn.summary: In a week shadowed by Kyiv missile strikes, Hormuz tensions, and geopolitical brinkmanship, UC Santa Barbara researchers quietly solved one of clean energy's hardest puzzles: storing sunlight not in clunky batteries, but inside molecules themselves — releasing the heat hours or days later, on demand. This kind of molecular solar storage has been a holy grail for decades, and a working proof-of-concept changes what's possible for heating homes, cooking food, and decarbonizing energy in places the grid can't reach. It won't make tomorrow's headlines less grim, but it may well power the world that comes after.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: UC Santa Barbara's liquid solar battery demonstrates genuine long-duration thermal storage using molecular chemistry — a meaningful leap beyond conventional photovoltaics.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Solar thermal storage without rare-earth metals or lithium could accelerate decarbonization in regions with abundant sun but fragile grid infrastructure.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: The Trump-Xi summit, despite no headline deals, kept dialogue channels open between the world's two largest economies during an active regional conflict.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Three Florida teenagers stopped to change a stranger's tire and ended up saving his life — a small, unambiguous act of decency that needed no geopolitical context.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: The Supreme Court maintained abortion pill telehealth access, preserving a concrete health option for millions while the broader legal battle continues.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Ground-level view looking straight up from a sun-baked California rooftop, camera pressed flat against warm terracotta tiles, sky filling three-quarters of the frame in deep teal-to-amber gradient. A young woman in casual clothes sits cross-legged on the rooftop edge above us, silhouetted from below against the blazing late-afternoon sun, cradling a small glass vial in both hands raised toward the light — the vial catching the sun and refracting a cone of molten gold downward directly into the camera lens. Her posture is reverent, elbows on knees, head tilted back slightly, the posture of someone holding something impossible and ordinary at once. Around her feet visible from below: a worn notebook splayed open, a thermos of water catching the same amber light. The rooftop solar panels behind her create a receding grid of dark rectangles against the sky, their edges glinting copper. Long dramatic shadows rake across the tiles toward us. The entire scene drenched in deep golden hour amber with teal sky above, radiant light bleeding around the silhouetted figure as though the sun is being drawn down into that single small vial she holds.
